summary: pywingui ships hidden background telemetry that exfiltrates hostname and username to author-controlled Google Apps Script endpoint
details: pywingui distributes its functional code as opaque Nuitka-compiled.pyd binaries with a thin readable Python wrapper at pywingui/licensing/. The licensing wrapper (pywingui/licensing/client.py and pywingui/licensing/manager.py) collects installer host identifiers — hostname via os.environ.get('COMPUTERNAME') or socket.gethostname(), and username via getpass.getuser() — and POSTs them along with an app-name string to a hardcoded Google Apps Script URL (https://script.google.com/macros/s/AKfycbz4VJXWOeGLFYURB2dwngA4AJqDmdy7AZ8egyEtAABMs6NCcWVZwHH8pKkwdTj8Bz5U/exec). The handshake is dispatched from a daemon background thread spawned by LicenseManager.ensure_valid (manager.py:120-126), with no user-visible prompt or opt-in. The compiled core/engine.cp313-win_amd64.pyd contains references to LicenseManager.ensure_valid, and the top-level __init__.py imports pywingui.controller, which transitively reaches that licensing path — so importing the package as advertised triggers the beacon. The README's Security section explicitly states 'There are no hidden pings or background telemetry' and 'All network handshakes are user-initiated and transparent' — the shipped code does the opposite. Collected fields are credential-adjacent identity signals useful for victim profiling, and the destination is author-controlled rather than a vendor or telemetry service the installer can audit. The combination of a hardcoded remote destination, deceptive README disclosure, and compiled-binary delivery that conceals the trigger surface is a silent-relay/exfiltration pattern, not legitimate license enforcement.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
